original_abstract:
The integration of visible light with enzymatic catalysis, termed photobiocatalysis, has recently emerged as a transformative frontier in sustainable organic synthesis. By leveraging the high selectivity of biocatalysts alongside the unique reactivity and energy profiles of photoexcited states, researchers have expanded the chemical space accessible to enzymes beyond their native biological functions. This review provides a comprehensive overview of state-of-the-art photobiocatalytic strategies reported through 2022, systematically categorizing methodologies based on the relationship between the photochemical event and the enzymatic transformation.

Three primary frameworks are delineated: photoenzymatic catalysis, where active-site cofactors are directly excited to initiate radical-based mechanisms; synergistic photoenzymatic catalysis, which utilizes exogenous photocatalysts to enable electron or energy transfer within a protein scaffold; and photobiocatalytic cascades, where independent light-driven reactions facilitate cofactor regeneration or in situ substrate modification. Each section details the diverse reaction mechanisms involved—such as the formation of charge-transfer complexes, hydrogen atom transfer, and single-electron transfer—while highlighting advancements in protein engineering and directed evolution tailored to these non-natural functions. Additionally, the review covers the use of whole-cell cyanobacterial systems as light-driven redox factories for green synthesis. By defining these distinct mechanistically grounded categories, this review aims to provide a clear conceptual framework for the field and illustrate the future potential of using light to unlock novel biocatalytic reactivity.
humanized_abstract:
In 2018, visible light and enzymatic catalysis were integrated together as photobiocatalysis in sustainable organic synthesis. By combining the selectivity of biocatalysts with the additional selectivity offered by photoexcited states and different reactivity, functions other than their biological role in living organisms can be achieved. This review provides a summary on recent advancements up to 2022 concerning the photobiocatalytic methods and is presenting them on the basis of the association between the photochemical trigger and the enzymatic reaction.

The book chapters identify three distinct photosynthesis independent mechanisms of action named photoenzymatic catalysis in which cofactors bound in the active sites of enzymes are excited and catalysis proceeds through radical intermediates (Chapter 5), synergistic photoenzymatic catalysis in which exogenous photocatalysts contribute to the electron/energy transfer processes within the protein (Chapter 6), and photobiocatalytic cascades in which photochemistry within the catalyst system serves to recycle cofactors or to alter in situ the substrate prior to biocatalytic transformation (Chapter 7). Each chapter provides details on the possible catalytic processes: charge transfer complex formation, hydrogen atom transfer, single electron transfer; related protein engineering and directed evolution methods to introduce non-natural functionalities; and includes a chapter on biocatalytic applications of whole-cell cyanobacteria as light driven redox factories for green synthesis catalysts. By defining the photosynthetic-independent mechanisms of action through mechanistically-based categories, this comprehensive volume on photocatalysis should not only provide a conceptual advance for photosynthesis-based biocatalysis but also demonstrate the great possibilities of the use of light for the introduction of new biocatalytic activities.
